title: Crucible Health: inference latency spike + transient 5xx on /v1/generate (INC-9821)
preview:
Customer escalation relayed by Stephanie Nguyen. Crucible Health reported sudden latency spike and burst of 5xx on inference endpoint /v1/generate beginning ~2026-08-28 07:55 PDT. Reported impact: median latency increased from ~120ms to ~900ms; throughput dropped from ~250 req/s to ~150 req/s for Crucible production key; initial customer-observed 5xx rate ~8% during the window; multiple batches timed out and downstream throttles triggered. Customer had a critical demo at 10:00 PDT.

Immediate hypotheses (serving): autoscaler oscillation under burst + degraded single shard causing head-of-line blocking.

Immediate mitigations executed: emergency scale-up of dedicated pool (target +20% capacity) + temporary concurrency limit increase + drain suspected bad instance(s) + enable routing of new requests to compatible fallback model variant (lower context) to reduce per-token work.

Outcome (updated after investigation): improvements observed within ~9–12 minutes; throughput returned to baseline and tail latency normalized after draining the degraded node(s) and reverting autoscaler policy.

Updated root cause (post-incident): the triggering event was an autoscaler policy update that reduced stabilization/cooldown and interacted poorly with a burst in queue depth, causing scale oscillation. One GPU instance concurrently entered a degraded driver state (intermittent kernel launch stalls rather than a hard OOM), amplifying tail latency and increasing request timeouts which surfaced as 5xx at the gateway. The instance replacement lag plus oscillation extended the incident duration.

Impact (finalized): peak 5xx rate revised to ~6–8% (varied by minute) with ~10–12 minute period of elevated errors; throughput dipped to ~60% of baseline at trough. No evidence of data integrity or security issues.

title: SEV-2: eu-west latency spike after runtime build 2026.03.18.0 (kernel selection)
preview:
Issue summary: p95 latency regression for long-context traffic in eu-west after runtime build 2026.03.18.0.

Impact: SLO burn for chat long-context bucket; customers saw slower responses but low error rate.

Environment: prod eu-west, A100 pools.

Investigation notes: Suspected kernel selection heuristic change; perf-canary shows regression aligned with build.

Mitigation: Disable heuristic via feature flag; force kernel to prior stable variant.

title: Device attestation key rotation breaks CLI session binding for internal devtools
preview:
Issue summary:
A recent rotation of the device attestation signing key (identity-service) appears to have invalidated existing CLI session bindings. Developer CLI clients receive repeated MFA prompts and then a hard 'session mismatch' error that prevents access to multiple internal tools (devtools-cli, runbook-runner, internal-grafana-proxy).

Impact:
- ~45 engineers unable to run infra CLI commands or access runbook tooling from corporate devices.
- Automated runbooks triggered from CI intermittently fail due to inability to exchange ephemeral session tokens.
- No broad user-facing customer impact, but critical for oncall and incident response workflows.

Environment:
- Prod control plane (identity-service) in us-west-2.
- Affected clients primarily macOS and Linux corporate laptops with device-attestation enabled.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Attempt to login with devtools-cli from a corporate laptop with device attestation enabled.
2) Complete MFA challenge when prompted.
3) Observe repeated MFA prompt cycles followed by: 'ERROR: session binding mismatch: attestation-signature-verified=false' and 401 from cli-proxy.

Expected behavior:
- Attestation signing key rotation should be seamless: new key should be trusted by the control plane and clients bound to prior attestation should either be re-chained automatically or receive a clear recovery prompt.

Actual behavior:
- Clients loop on MFA then fail with session binding mismatch. Manual logout and login sometimes works after several attempts but not consistently.

Logs / error excerpts:
- cli-proxy: "WARN session_verifier: signature_check_failed key_id=attest-2026-03-10 reason=unknown_format"
- identity-service: "ERROR attestation: signature-validation failed for device=host-2345 status=untrusted-key"
- devtools-cli client: "session binding mismatch (attestation-signature-verified=false)
  token exchange failed: 401"

Temporary workaround:
- Some engineers can bypass device-attestation by using a personal device (not allowed by policy for many oncall flows).
- Support can issue a one-time session token via internal support console for immediate access (manual, rate limited).

Next steps requested / acceptance criteria:
- Determine whether new signing key was rolled out without updating the control plane truststore or if clients are sending an old key reference.
- Reproduce on staging and identify minimal rollback or hotfix path that restores session binding for corporate devices.
- Provide mitigation plan that restores oncall access within 4 hours or an agreed mitigation that allows runbook-triggering from CI.

title: SCIM group displayName with emoji prevents RBAC mapping for internal console elevations
preview:
Issue summary:
Several internal users cannot assume temporary elevated roles in the support console after being added to Okta groups whose displayName contains non-ASCII characters (emoji and mixed scripts). This surfaced after an HR group import that included emojis and slashes in group names.

Impact:
- Engineers could not escalate to runbook/incident responder roles for two oncall incidents; manual escalation caused ~10-20 minute delays.
- Affects any group where SCIM displayName includes surrogate pairs or combined unicode sequences. ASCII-only groups unaffected.

Environment:
- Okta -> SCIM sync -> internal identity-proxy (us-west).
- RBAC mapper resolves Okta group displayName to internal role grants during elevation.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create Okta group with displayName: "Incident Responders 🚨" and externalId: "incident-responders".
2) Allow SCIM incremental sync.
3) Attempt temporary elevation in support console bound to that group.
4) Observe 403 and missing mapping in rbac-mapper logs.

Observed behavior:
- rbac-mapper raises UnicodeNormalizationError while normalizing displayName.
- identity-proxy includes group claim with JSON-escaped surrogate pairs; downstream equality check fails.
- Console shows a generic "Access denied" with no normalization hint.

Expected behavior:
- RBAC mapping should use externalId as canonical mapping or reliably normalize displayName so group grants are deterministic regardless of unicode content.

title: Suspicious mass auth attempts after internal "job application" form; possible credential harvest impacting service accounts
preview:
Summary: On 2026-03-13 09:42 UTC the security monitoring alerting pipeline flagged a clustered spike of failed and then some successful token exchanges from several service accounts following a widely-circulated internal "job application" Google Form. The pattern suggests credentials may have been harvested via the form and subsequently used to attempt programmatic access.

Impact: Multiple service-account API tokens and a small number of CI systems observed anomalous auth activity. No confirmed data exfiltration detected so far. Some internal CI jobs failed due to token rotation triggered after suspicious logins. Customer-facing services are not known to be affected.

Detection: Real-time SIEM rules (auth-anomaly-001) and the anomaly detector for token-exchange rate detected a burst between 09:25–09:47 UTC with (>120) failed refreshes and 7 successful refreshes originating from 3 public IPs not previously seen in the org. Alert fired to #security and #oncall.

Initial hypothesis: Public Google Form (internal-only link accidentally made public) was used to collect resumes; attackers embedded a credential-harvesting redirect or 3rd-party widget that captured employee credentials. Harvested credentials then used to authenticate service principals (shared secrets stored in CI) or to exchange for short-lived tokens via our IdP.

Immediate containment actions taken:
- Rotated affected service-account keys and CI secrets for flagged projects (automated rotations executed 09:55–10:20 UTC).
- Blocked originating IPs at the cloud firewall and added them to blocklist in WAF (completed 10:05 UTC).
- Disabled the public job form and removed external sharing; notified GSuite admin to revoke any third-party app consents.
- Temporarily enforced stricter token issuance policy for our IdP for 30m window (reduced refresh window).

Next steps (short term):
- Complete audit of all service accounts that had token activity during the window (Owner: IAM team).
- Run credential exposure checks against recent form submissions (Owner: Security Ops).
- Identify whether any of the 7 successful token exchanges correspond to privilege escalation actions in Cloud Audit Logs.
- Revoke any suspicious sessions and ensure CI pipelines use rotated secrets.

Longer-term follow-ups:
- Review internal form sharing policies and update onboarding docs to require security review for externally embedded widgets.
- Add a SIEM rule to detect sudden token-exchange success rate following a high volume of failed refreshes.
- Postmortem and employee notification plan if any account compromise confirmed.

Contact: Security Ops (pager), Oncall SRE, Identity team.

title: Centralize ephemeral labeling-seat pool for LabelWave pilot (SCIM + BYO-KMS) and align renewal to FYQ3
preview:
Issue summary:
We need to onboard vendor 'LabelWave' for a 6-week labeling pilot. The vendor requires: SCIM provisioning for ephemeral contractor accounts, BYO-KMS support for storing exported label artifacts, and a single invoice aligned to our FY Q3 renewal window.

Impact:
- Research & Data team cannot start the pilot until seat provisioning is available.
- Contractors currently require manual invites which increases onboarding time by ~2 days per contractor and creates security auditing gaps.
- If renewal dates are not aligned, we will incur multiple pro-rated invoices and extra procurement overhead.

Request / Acceptance criteria:
1) Create a centralized ephemeral license pool (50 seats) that can be handed out to contractors and reclaimed automatically via SCIM deprovisioning.
2) Configure SCIM integration with LabelWave and map attributes: email, manager, cost_center tag.
3) Validate BYO-KMS workflow (AWS KMS key ARN provided by Redwood) — ensure LabelWave can encrypt exported artifacts using our key or confirm an approved alternative.
4) Finance: route invoices to Cost Center: RDT-40 and consolidate renewal to align with Redwood FY Q3 start date (2026-07-01).
5) Security: run a short intake checklist (SaaS security questionnaire + SOC2 evidence) and provide a go/no-go.
6) Deliver a short runbook for on-call and CS to manage seat reclamation and emergency access.

Attachments (uploaded to ticket): contract_draft_LabelWave_2026-03-08.pdf; vendor_security_answers_LabelWave_2026-03-09.xlsx

title: Restricted forensic sample extract for privacy-incident reproduction
preview:
Issue summary:
A customer reported a potential PII leak in generated outputs from an application using redwood/longform-v1. We need a narrowly scoped forensic sample extract from production inference logs to reproduce the issue and validate whether PII appeared in model outputs.

Justification:
- Reproduction required to determine root cause (model prompt leakage vs. prompt content).
- Small, time-boxed sample minimizes exposure.

Scope of data requested:
- Time window: 2026-03-11 00:00:00 to 2026-03-11 23:59:59 (UTC)
- Up to 200 inference records matching the customer tenant and the reported conversation hashes.
- Include fields: request_id, customer_tenant_id (hashed), request_prompt (redacted), response_text (original and redacted copy), model_version, routing_info, timestamp, and system metadata (instance id, region).
- Explicit exclusion: do NOT include raw user identifiers or customer-provided file blobs.

Redaction & security controls proposed:
- Hash customer_tenant_id with HMAC-SHA256 and store salt in KMS-scoped key; original id not stored in extract.
- Run automated PII DLP scanner on response_text and mask email addresses, phone numbers, and long (>=16) numeric sequences. Keep an unmasked copy in an encrypted S3 object encrypted with customer-specific KMS key accessible only to approvers for a maximum of 7 days.
- Write-only S3 path with ACL restricted to the approved list; enable S3 object lock and server-side encryption with KMS.
- Access audit: all downloads require an IAM session with MFA and are logged to the security audit trail.

Retention & disposal:
- Extract TTL: 7 calendar days from extraction completion; automated deletion triggered by lifecycle rule.
- Any derived artifacts (screenshots, local copies) must be ephemeral and deleted after use; users must attest to deletion in this ticket.

Approval needed from:
- Security (data handling and key access)
- Legal/Privacy (customer notice & contract review)
- Engineering (SRE or runtime owner to run job)

Impact if delayed:
- Slows customer response and may escalate to incident; potential regulatory exposure if PII is confirmed.

Requested owner and run plan:
- Owner: Ethan Park (SRE) to run extraction job with help from Infra tooling.
- Run plan: run a narrow query against tensorflow-prod-logs table, filter by customer tenant hash, limit 200 rows, pipe to DLP scanner, package results to secure S3 with metadata manifest and HMAC verification.

Acceptance criteria:
- Security and Legal sign-off documented in comments.
- Extract contains only scoped fields and passes DLP masking policy.
- TTL and audit controls in place and verified.

title: Provisioning/Repair: Fleet power policy rollout locks CPU at low P-state causing Windows container start failures
preview:
Issue summary:
A recent Intune-driven corporate power policy rollout applied to a subset of developer and infra workstations appears to be forcing minimum processor states and other AC power parameters that lock CPU performance at the lowest P-state on some systems. Affected machines show sustained ~400 MHz CPU frequency on AC power after provisioning/re-imaging which causes Windows containers (Docker Desktop and Windows Server containers used for local dev) to fail health checks and refuse to start reliably.

Impact:
- Several internal engineers and platform developers cannot start local containers; CI jobs that rely on Windows container hosts fail on self-hosted runners on affected machines.
- Issue observed on ~12 confirmed devices in the us-west fleet; likely more unreported.
- Productivity impact (blocked local dev, intermittent CI failures). No customer-facing outage.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 22H2 (corporate image)

title: TTPO deprovision: staggered SSO retirement, MDM re-enroll, and courier-hold for cross-border departure
preview:
Issue summary:
HR has requested a two-week temporary passover (TTPO) deprovision for an employee relocating overseas mid-contract. Request is to stagger account deactivation so service accounts and SSO remain available for knowledge-transfer tasks until the courier confirms laptop collection. HR flagged export/compliance concerns for the laptop transit and asked Asset Management to place a hold with the courier.

Impact:
- Potential risk of accidental early SSO/account revocation interrupting handover work (engineering + documentation updates).
- Hardware custody must remain in chain-of-custody due to encryption keys tied to device KMS tags.
- Cross-border courier requires explicit KYC and export paperwork before release.

Environment:
- Internal accounts: SSO (Okta), GSuite, GitHub org, AWS console role.
- Device managed by MDM (Jamf).
- Employee tier: full-time engineering (access to staging and prod service accounts).

Requested outcome:
1) Create a hard stop plan that defers destructive deprovisioning actions for two weeks (TTPO window) and documents which identities are delayed and which are removed immediately.
2) Coordinate with Asset Management to create a courier hold (do not release) with a named carrier and tracking, including export paperwork checklist.
3) Re-enroll the device into a custody MDM profile if courier requires device reset prior to shipping.
4) Schedule final deprovision (full SSO removal, KMS key rotation, service-account handback) upon confirmation of device receipt and HR sign-off.

Background and constraints:
- Employee will be physically leaving the country on 2026-03-20; HR requested a TTPO window through 2026-03-27 to finish handover tasks.
- Security policy requires device rekey when device leaves company control for >72 hours. We need to reconcile that with the TTPO exception.
- Legal requires export checklist for this country; Asset Management has initiated a case with logistics partner but needs support from Security and Compliance for documentation.

Attachments:
- HR exit form (attached in HR drive link)
- Logistics pre-check PDF (uploaded to ticket)

Notes for assignees: please avoid immediate deactivation of roles that would break CI or production runbooks; instead rotate service account keys where possible and freeze destructive IAM role deprovision until final closure.

title: Okta SAML session affinity breaks cross-account console switching
preview: Issue summary: Several engineers and on-call have reported that when they use the console's 'Switch account' feature after authenticating via Okta SAML, they are immediately redirected back to the Okta login flow or see a 401 in the console even though Okta indicates an active session. This appears to be a session affinity / cookie routing problem that surfaces only when switching between org accounts (cross-account switch) or when requests are routed to different backends behind the auth-proxy.

title: Temporary telemetry query privilege for data-minimization audit
preview:
Issue summary:
Request from Compliance (Privacy) to run a constrained set of telemetry queries against production request logs to confirm that new data-minimization rules have reduced retention of user identifiers in prefix caches. This is not an export request; Compliance needs aggregated metrics and a small number of sampled rows to validate masking rules.

Background:
- New data-minimization policy shipped 2026-03-05 adjusted KV cache retention and redaction on session keys.
- Compliance requires a short verification window to confirm no PII leakage in the last 7 days of production telemetry.

Impact / Risk:
- High sensitivity: production logs contain request metadata and potentially user-provided content.
- Goal is to avoid broad access to raw payloads and ensure least-privilege.

Requested access:
- JIT (just-in-time) query privilege for Compliance engineer: Duration 4 hours, single session, scoped to aggregated queries + up to 10 sampled rows with deterministic redaction applied.
- Queries will run through the observability query-console behind bastion with session recording and enforced masking UDFs.

Justification and minimality plan:
- Compliance will only need: counts, distinct user_id counts, percentage of requests containing raw email patterns, and up to 10 sampled requests where identifiers are replaced with stable tokens (tokenization) and free-text redaction applied.
- No full payload dumps, no key-value cache export. If any sample row contains unexpected PII after redaction, session is terminated and incident escalated.

Access controls and safeguards requested:
- Role granted: telemetry_read_jit (timebound IAM role that maps to a short-lived certificate).
- Network: access only via secure bastion (AUDIT-LOGS flagged), from Compliance laptop (registered device).
- Query pre-approval: Compliance to provide queries in advance. Only pre-approved queries allowed; ad-hoc queries blocked by policy.
- Data transformations: apply built-in redaction UDFs to sampled rows and mask user identifiers in the result set.
- Audit: all queries and results captured in the audit trail; copies retained for 90 days in encrypted bucket.

Duration and rollback:
- Access window: 2026-03-16 09:00 to 2026-03-16 13:00 US/Eastern (4 hours).
- If any policy violation or unexpected PII is observed, access revoked immediately and IR lead notified.

Acceptance criteria / Success criteria:
- Compliance obtains aggregated metrics showing reduction in identifier retention according to the new policy across the last 7 days.
- Sampled rows (max 10) show tokenized identifiers and redacted free text.
- No raw payloads or unredacted identifiers are exported.

Attachments (queries to run):
1) COUNT(*) by day for last 7 days with prefix-cache-hits filter.
2) DISTINCT(user_id) count and histogram of session length.
3) Regex-based scan for email patterns; return counts and top-5 matching request IDs (masked).
4) Return up to 10 sampled rows after redaction UDF applied (sampling seeded for reproducibility).

Next steps requested from SRE/Security:
- Create timebound IAM role and JIT certificate for the compliance principal.
- Pre-register the bastion session with recording enabled and notify IR lead.
- Validate redaction UDFs with a dry-run on staging using synthetic data prior to production run.

title: GCP Workload Identity federation -> AWS assume-role: session-tag truncation causing KMS key policy denies during Dedicated provisioning
preview:
Issue summary:
During automated Dedicated capacity provisioning for customer-tenant 'acme-ml', the cross-cloud credential broker (GCP Workload Identity Federation -> AWS STS) succeeded but downstream KMS operations failed with AccessDenied. Impact: Dedicated provisioner could not finish creating reserved clusters for two customers, blocking onboarding and test traffic.

Impact:
- 2 Dedicated provisioning runs failed in us-east region (customer-facing).
- Provisioner left partial resources (EC2 instances created without required KMS-wrapped secrets).
- Oncall had to rollback an attempted retry causing extra cost.

Environment:
- Provisioner service: prod-us-east namespace
- Broker: gcp-wif-broker (uses service account svc-provisioner@redwood-prod.iam.gserviceaccount.com)
- AWS role: arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/redwood-dedicated-provisioner
- KMS key: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:222222222222:key/aa11-bb22-cc33

Expected behavior:
- Broker issues STS token with session tags (team, tenant, request_id); AWS assume-role propagates tags and KMS key policy allows Encrypt/Decrypt when aws:PrincipalTag/team == "platform" or tenant == "acme-ml".

Observed behavior:
- STS assume-role succeeded (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) but subsequent KMS Encrypt calls return AccessDenied: "User: arn:aws:sts::222222222222:assumed-role/redwood-dedicated-provisioner/session is not authorized to perform: kms:Encrypt on resource ... because condition aws:PrincipalTag/tenant missing"

title: Windows 11 roaming + adapter power-save causing WireGuard route ghosting and DNS failures on corporate Wi‑Fi
preview:
Issue summary:
Windows 11 laptops intermittently lose connectivity to internal services after roaming between APs. The WireGuard interface remains up but the system installs a stale 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing at the local wifi gateway (metric low), effectively blackholing outbound traffic for processes bound to the wg interface. DNS queries either fail or go to the wrong resolver.

Impact:
- ~8 active users in the SF office reporting repeated failures during heavy roaming (moving between floors / boardroom).
- Symptoms: applications time out (internal dashboards, git pushes), developer shells lose connections, Slack remains connected but messages to internal services fail.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 22H2 (build 22631.x)
- Wireless chipset: Intel AX211 / AX201 (driver 22.50.1 reported on affected machines)
- VPN: WireGuard v0.7.6 (enterprise config via enterprise-agent)
- Zero-trust/agent: redwood-edge v2.1.0 (manages wg interface and DNS overrides)
- SSID: Redwood-Corp (802.11ax, controller-managed fast-roaming/FT enabled)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Connect to Redwood-Corp from a Windows 11 laptop with WireGuard active.
2) Walk between two nearby APs or trigger a roam using the vendor utility (force roam).
3) Observe that user retains WPA2/WPA3 session but some processes lose access to internal services.
4) Run 'wg show', 'route print', and 'nslookup internal-service'.

Observed behavior / logs (excerpt):
- route print shows 0.0.0.0 -> 192.168.10.1 metric 10 (preferred) coexisting with wg0 route 0.0.0.0 -> 10.10.0.1 metric 50; processes bound to wg0 still attempt outbound but packets never egress correctly.
- WireGuard peers show keepalives delayed; 'wg show' shows handshake timestamps stall for 30–120s.
- Windows System event: WLAN AutoConfig reported 'Roaming completed' then 'Driver queued packets during power save transition'.
- tcpdump on edge gateway shows missing keepalives during DTIM handover windows.

Initial hypothesis:
- Wireless driver power-management / DTIM handling during fast roaming causes packet queuing and delayed keepalives; WireGuard's endpoint detection thinks tunnel still healthy but routing table gets re-ordered when interface metrics are recalculated.
- Controller fast-roaming timers (FT) combined with Windows power profile and driver implementation create a race where the default route is re-inserted before wg interface routes stabilize.

Impact assessment:
- P1 due to multiple developers blocked during peak hours; intermittent but reproducible in SF office.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced on 2 lab machines with same Intel AX211 driver.
- Disabling 'Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power' on the WLAN adapter immediately eliminated the symptom on test devices.
- Changing Windows Power Plan (Wireless Adapter Settings -> Power Saving Mode = Maximum Performance) also mitigated.
- On the controller side, reducing FT roam aggressiveness and increasing reassociation hold time reduced the frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Packet captures show keepalives delayed by up to 90s during roam; WireGuard handshake resumes but application sockets experience default TCP timeouts.
- Intune profile was used to push temporary registry settings and wireless power plan to a pilot group (10 machines) with success.

Mitigation applied:
- Deployed Intune device configuration to pilot cohort (10 users in SF) to set Wireless Adapter power to Maximum Performance and to disable the 'allow device to sleep' option.
- Rolled controller config change to throttle FT handoffs to be less aggressive (increase reassociation timer to 500ms).
- Sent bug report to Intel with packet captures and driver logs; vendor acknowledged potential driver-side race and requested additional telemetry.

Next steps / plan:
- Expand Intune mitigation to all SF office laptops (scheduled phased rollout over 3 days).
- Validate Intel driver fix when available; test in lab (matrix: Windows builds x driver versions x WG config).
- Add a customer-side guard in redwood-edge agent: detect roaming handshake gap > 10s and force reassertion of wg route metrics (work queued as GH PR).
- Update wireless runbook with mitigation steps and add monitoring rule for 'wg handshake gap' on fleet.

Related artifacts and links:
- Packet captures: s3://redwood-captures/INT-864209/roam-pcap-0309.pcap (internal)
- Controller config change PR: https://git.internal.redwood.net/network/configs/pull/122

title: Intermittent path MTU blackhole with 464XLAT/CLAT causing VPN tunnel TLS stalls to internal k8s API
preview:
Issue summary: Several on-prem and remote employees intermittently lose connectivity to internal Kubernetes control-plane endpoints (k8s API) over the corporate VPN. Symptoms include HTTPS stalls (TLS connection hung after ClientHello/ServerHello), kubectl timeouts, and SSO failures when Okta redirects attempt to hit cluster endpoints.

Impact: Affects ~8% of remote users on IPv6-only mobile hotspots and some office users routed through a branch edge running CLAT/464XLAT for IPv6-only carrier links. Breaks developer workflows (kubectl, code pushes) and automated jobs that depend on k8s API. No evidence of a serving platform outage; problem correlates with path MTU size and IPv6-to-IPv4 translation path.

Environment: Production, corporate VPN (WireGuard-based branch/host tunnels), edge NAT64/464XLAT devices at two branch sites (boston-edge, seattle-edge). Affected clients: macOS 14 and Android hotspots. Control-plane endpoint IP: 10.20.5.12 (internal VPC).

Observed behavior: TLS handshakes appear to complete in some packet captures but subsequent HTTP traffic stalls. tracepath/traceroute6 show MTU clamping on the path when CLAT is present; larger packets (1480+) are dropped without ICMPv6 Packet Too Big messages being delivered to the client. The VPN uses UDP encapsulation and fragmentation is disabled on clients (df set).

Expected behavior: PMTU discovery should work across translated paths. Larger packets should either be fragmented/PMTU-adjusted or ICMPv6/PTB should be forwarded so endpoints can reduce MSS and avoid stalls.

title: Phased offboarding for senior engineer: partial SSO deprovision delay, expiring bridge service-account, and corporate mobile return logistics
preview:
Issue summary: Senior SWE (employee# 92014) is transitioning from full-time to a 6-week contractor bridge starting 2026-03-15. HR requests a phased offboarding rather than immediate deprovisioning because the engineer will complete two critical rollups: (1) a code-owner handoff for the inference scheduler repo and (2) a multi-region migration playbook.

Impact: Immediate full deprovisioning would block final rollouts and risk a regression in production. Delaying some access increases risk surface and requires controlled, auditable exceptions (temporary service account + expiring keys).

Request: 1) Keep SSO and repo write access for two weeks (until 2026-03-29) but remove access to billing consoles and dedicated GPU lab immediately. 2) Create a temporary bridge service-account scoped to the inference-scheduler repo and the release pipeline with keys that expire 2026-03-29. 3) Reassign code-owner and create a PR for ownership changes before 2026-03-18. 4) Coordinate physical asset return: company phone must be shipped back to UK office (return window 2026-03-20 to 2026-03-24) and laptop to be couriered to HQ. 5) Security/Legal sign-off required for exception; provide audit trail.

Environment: prod (eu-west), infra components touched: IAM, KMS, GitHub, asset-inventory, courier vendor (DHL), HR caseworker queue.

Policy question: Does Redwood allow a timeboxed SSO delay with a scoped, expiring service account for a contractor bridge? What approving authority is required and which retention/audit records are mandatory for the exception?

title: Microphone fails to enumerate after BIOS flash + Secure Boot toggle during depot repair
preview:
Issue summary: After a standard depot BIOS flash followed by toggling Secure Boot to re-enable legacy imaging paths, attached device microphones (internal array) no longer enumerate in Windows. Observed on small subset of fleet following recent vendor BIOS (v1.18.4) rollout to depot units.

Impact: Affected users see "No audio input device installed" in Settings and Device Manager; internal microphone missing from audio input list. External USB microphones still function. This blocks users from joining voice calls and impacts QA signoff for a subset of refurbished laptops. Approximately 7 confirmed units to date, all processed through the same depot workflow.

Environment: Depot repair/flash workflow, corp provisioning image (Win11-23H2-corp), Secure Boot toggled from Enabled -> Disabled -> Enabled during repair validation; BIOS version 1.18.4 on affected units.

Observed behavior: After reboot, Windows audio subsystem loads but Microphone Array device does not appear in Device Manager; no driver nodes for the audio DSP. Windows Event Log shows Service Control Manager entries for audioendpointbuilder failing to start dependent driver with Code 7000. Kernel logs show ACPI _DSM calls returning different hardware IDs after Secure Boot toggle.

Expected behavior: Internal microphone enumerates and appropriate audio codec driver (vendor) is present under Sound, video and game controllers and Audio inputs and outputs.

Notes: This ticket is an internal support/depot-repair workflow issue; not customer-facing. Triage requested to determine whether the BIOS change altered firmware visibility or audio codec microcontroller (DSP) firmware got re-flashed/disabled when Secure Boot state changed.

title: UK BACS payment rejected: beneficiary address missing on vendor bank record causing payment hold
preview:
Issue summary:
A scheduled UK BACS payment to vendor 'Northbridge Components Ltd' was rejected by the bank with return code RM124 (beneficiary address missing). Payment was submitted on 2026-03-09 as part of the weekly AP run and entered 'Rejected by bank' status in the payments ledger.

Impact:
- Vendor invoice for PO PO-99877 (net £40,000) plus VAT remains unpaid; vendor has flagged potential shipment hold if not cleared within 3 business days.
- Affects one high-value supplier on monthly cadence.
- Treasury team unable to reprocess until beneficiary details are updated in vendor master and bank resubmission performed.

Environment:
- Payments system: Payments Portal v3.4 (production)
- Bank channel: UK BACS via Banking Partner (Clearline Payments)
- Region: eu-west-2 (London)

Steps to reproduce / observed sequence:
1) AP run triggered 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC for UK suppliers.
2) Payments Portal generated BACS file and transmitted to Clearline.
3) Bank acknowledged receipt, then returned the transaction with RM124.
4) Payments Portal logged rejection and flagged payment for manual intervention.

Attachments / Logs:
- Payments Portal audit trail ID: pay-20260309-99877
- Bank return message (Clearline): "RM124 - Beneficiary address incomplete/missing" (see attached bank XML in ticket attachments).

Requested action:
- Confirm missing beneficiary address fields and update vendor master record.
- Coordinate with treasury to reissue payment (manual or batch amend) and confirm routing.
- Notify vendor of expected reissue time and confirm no shipment interruption.

title: Cleanup and reconcile SLA orchestration: template variables, routing keys, and reporting parity
preview:
Issue summary:
Recent deploys to sla-enforcer-v2 and the rule-engine refactor created several small divergences between SLA configuration, routing key normalization, and the downstream reporting transforms. The net effect: daily SLA breach counts in the support dashboard are ~7% lower than expected and some internal routing macros do not expand, causing tickets to be auto-assigned to the default queue instead of tiered on-call.

Impact:
- Incorrect auto-assignment for ~120 internal support tickets last week (internal queues).
- Daily SLA breach totals for internal dashboards are undercounted (~7% gap) which affects SRE on-call capacity planning.
- Risk of misrouted P1/P2 escalations if macros are not fixed before next release window.

Observed behavior / Evidence:
- Audit log from sla-enforcer (2026-03-08 03:14 - 03:46 UTC) shows macro expansion failures: variable \"priority_level\" not found; templates expect \"priority\".
- Rule-engine PR #1420 changed routing key normalization from kebab-case to snake_case; existing SLA configs reference kebab-case route keys (e.g., \"sla-high-latency\").
- Reporting pipeline transform (support-alerts consumer) drop observed for field \"sla_target_ms\" after schema evolution on Kafka topic support.alerts.v2 (see consumer offset gaps and schema registry diff).
- Slack thread: #internal-sla-tooling (2026-03-09) contains a reproduction trace and raw event samples.

Hypothesis / Root cause (working):
1) Template variable rename: a configuration migration in sla-enforcer-v2 introduced a canonical variable name \"priority_level\" while existing macros and templates used \"priority\"; the templating layer does not provide a backwards-compatibility alias.
2) Routing key normalization mismatch: rule-engine normalization change means configured routing rules no longer match incoming normalized keys unless configs are migrated.
3) Reporting transform schema drift: reporting consumer expects legacy schema and silently drops fields it doesn't find, producing undercounted metrics.

Immediate mitigation (short-term):
- Revert sla-enforcer template parsing to accept both \"priority\" and \"priority_level\" (hotfix branch prepared: support-tooling PR #1432).
- Add temporary adapter in rule-engine that canonicalizes incoming keys to both formats to avoid loss of matches (feature flag controlled).
- Patch reporting consumer to log dropped fields and emit counts to metrics for visibility while consumer is migrated.

Proposed long-term fix (plan):
- Full config migration script that rewrites existing routing keys to snake_case and updates macros to canonical variable names; add migration dry-run and rollback support.
- Add schema/version headers to support.alerts topics and upgrade reporting consumers with explicit schema evolution handling; add an alert when \"sla_target_ms\" is missing from >0.1% of messages.
- Add unit and integration tests that exercise macro expansion for both legacy and new templates and include these checks in the release pipeline.

Acceptance criteria:
- Macro expansion error rate drops to <0.01% for the next 7 days in prod.
- Routing miss rate due to key normalization drops to zero after migration (validated by matched/unmatched routing metrics).
- Daily SLA counts in dashboard reconcile to within 0.5% of ground-truth sampling (manual audit) for two consecutive days.

Next steps / Actions assigned:
- Mateo Alvarez: apply hotfix to sla-enforcer and enable feature flag for adapter in rule-engine (ETA 2026-03-12).
- Priya Mehta (Reporting): deploy patch to reporting consumer to surface dropped-field metrics and run backfill on 2026-03-11's topic window.
- Leah Patel: author config migration script and schedule maintenance window; create rollback plan and confluence doc.

Follow-ups / Observability playbook additions:
- Add a dedicated Grafana panel for template expansion error rate and quota for the support team.
- Create a runbook step to run the routing-key migration dry-run before any rule-engine normalization changes.
- Add a pre-deploy integration test that validates that the support console resolves macros against a canonical template set.

Priority and timeline rationale:
This is P2/Sev2 because the issue affects internal routing and metrics accuracy (operational visibility), but there is a straightforward hotfix and low immediate customer impact. The goal is to complete migration and validation before the next monthly deploy cycle.

title: Runner init fails: kubeconfig file mode changed by secrets-sync job, preventing runner startup for on-prem release fleet
preview:
Issue summary:
During the nightly secrets reconciliation (secrets-sync) we observed that the kubeconfig used by our self-hosted release runners in the on-prem release cluster had its file mode changed from 0640 to 0600. The runner systemd unit runs as user 'runner' and group 'ci', and relies on group-read to access /etc/runner/kubeconfig. After the mode change runners fail to start, fail health checks, and cannot pick up deployment jobs.

Impact:
- Nightly release jobs for several teams (release/* pipelines) failed to start between 2026-03-10 02:10 and 2026-03-10 03:40 UTC.
- ~22 CI jobs were queued and timed out, delaying a cross-functional canary promotion.
- No user-facing production outages, but release cadence blocked.

Environment:
- prod on-prem release cluster in our us-west colo.
- Runner fleet: self-hosted systemd runners (image: redwood/runner:2026.02.1).
- Secrets pipeline: ansible-driven secrets-sync job that runs hourly and re-applies kubeconfigs and vault-wrapped secrets.

Steps to reproduce:
1) On a runner host, inspect /etc/runner/kubeconfig file mode and ownership (expected: -rw-r----- runner:ci).
2) Trigger secrets-sync job with playbook 'deploy/k8s/kubeconfigs.yml' against release hosts.
3) Observe resulting file mode changed to -rw------- and systemd-runner fails to start with permission errors.

Observed behavior / logs:
- systemd journal from runner host: "src/runtime/init: failed to open kubeconfig /etc/runner/kubeconfig: permission denied"
- /var/log/messages: "Runner process (pid 4321) permission denied reading /etc/runner/kubeconfig"
- secrets-sync ansible task shows: 'copy: mode=0600 owner=root group=root dest=/etc/runner/kubeconfig' (playbook currently defaults to root:root/0600 when re-rendering templates)

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis: recent change in the secrets-sync playbook (PR #3746) introduced a conservative file mode default (0600) for generated kubeconfigs; previously we applied 0640 with group 'ci'. The change was intended for security but didn't account for the runner service user model on on-prem hosts.
- Confirmed by reproducing the playbook run in staging: playbook sets mode=0600 and owner=root:root.
- Runners created after 2026-02-20 use a different startup script that drops privileges to runner:ci; older hosts had kubeconfigs managed by a different mechanism and were unaffected. The mix of host generation templates caused an inconsistent fleet state.
- No vault/KMS errors were observed; this is strictly a file-permissions regression from the deployment step.

Mitigation applied:
- Rollback: temporarily reverted deploy/k8s/kubeconfigs.yml change in secrets-sync (rolled back PR #3746 in the orchestration repo) at 2026-03-10 03:05 UTC.
- Escalated hotfix: executed a remediation ansible run to restore file mode and ownership across runner hosts: mode=0640 owner=root group=ci for /etc/runner/kubeconfig and restarted systemd runner service.
- Restarted ~120 runners; ~96 returned healthy within 7 minutes. Remaining hosts had unrelated disk pressure and require separate tickets.

Current status:
- Runners are recovering; release queue processing resumed at 2026-03-10 03:40 UTC.
- Change reverted in secrets-sync, and a targeted remediation played across prod fleet completed.

Open questions and next steps:
1) Modify secrets-sync playbook to accept per-host ACL parameters and to set group 'ci' and 0640 mode for runner kubeconfig artifacts.
2) Add a pre-flight validation in the runner systemd unit that emits a clear metric/count when kubeconfig permissions are non-compliant so alarms trigger before job failures.
3) Add a CI gate that runs a lint/validation on secrets playbooks for file mode changes that could break services.
4) Perform an audit to find any other services relying on group-read for generated secrets.

Owner for remediation and follow-ups: Priya Nair (Release Eng) in collaboration with Secrets Ops (Diego Morales).

Related artifacts: see linked PRs and runbooks below.

title: Possible SSO session replay after employee clicked external form link
preview:
Issue summary: An employee clicked a link in an externally hosted vendor form received via email. Within one hour, anomalous session activity for that account was observed: short-lived logins from unfamiliar IPs and requests that matched session-reuse patterns. Impact: potential session token exposure and attempted session replay. Initial scope: single user account (product-ops@redwood.ai), possibly more if the external form captured session info via query params or third-party trackers.

Timeline:
- 2026-03-12 09:27 UTC: Employee reports odd redirect after clicking vendor form link (Support Slack #sec-alerts).
- 2026-03-12 09:34 UTC: Proxy logs show outbound request to analytics.example-forms.com with a query string including a transient token-like value.
- 2026-03-12 09:45 UTC: Cloudflare auth logs show additional login events for product-ops@redwood.ai from two foreign IPs (AS12345) within a 10-minute window.
- 2026-03-12 10:10 UTC: Security detection flagged multiple short-lived sessions and created this ticket.

Environment: Production Redwood Console, us-east region, SSO via Okta (OIDC), corporate proxy (BlueGate) filtering egress.

Steps to reproduce (initial hypothesis):
1) User opens vendor email and clicks link to external hosted form (vendor domain: forms-vendor.com).
2) The hosted form includes third-party analytics (analytics.example-forms.com) which captures URL parameters.
3) A session identifier was appended to the redirect URL (possibly from the browser referrer or an incorrectly constructed omniauth return URL) and ended up in the analytics request.
4) Remote actor(s) replayed captured token, attempting to re-use the session against our auth endpoint.

Logs / evidence references:
- BlueGate proxy request: outbound GET to https://analytics.example-forms.com/collect?v=1&sid=eyJhbGci... (truncated) timestamp 2026-03-12T09:34:02Z (see proxy trace ID BG-8813).
- Cloudflare auth logs: login events for product-ops@redwood.ai from 185.62.44.10 and 185.62.44.11 at 09:45 UTC (location: offshore region); user agent: curl/7.85.0.
- Okta sessions API: session token for product-ops account was active from 09:25-09:48 UTC and then invalidated at 10:05 UTC.

Initial triage: Presumed session token leaked via URL parameter captured by third-party analytics. No evidence yet of privilege escalation or data exfil beyond attempted session reuse. We have temporarily revoked the user sessions and rotated API service tokens for the user's active ephemeral keys.

title: Consolidate Q3 SaaS seat forecast and run security + procurement evaluation for new annotation vendor
preview:
Issue summary:
- Request: Procure and onboard a small-pilot bundle of seats for a vendor evaluation (annotation tool) and reconcile Q3 seat forecast for Data Labeling and Research teams.
- Business impact: Labeling work for two product pilots (Search tuning and Safety evals) is blocked without a vetted annotation tool; delays will push model evaluation and release timelines by ~6 weeks.
- Requested scope: 25 initial editor seats + 5 manager seats (30 total) for 12 months (pilot -> expansion). Renewal cadence: annual.
- Vendor: LabelWave (SME annotation platform; SOC2 self-attested) — initial commercial quote attached in Confluence.
- Licensing model: per-seat annual subscription with optional storage add-on. Initial quoted cost: $36,000/yr (USD) for seats + $3,000 one-time migration assistance.
- Budget owner: ML Ops (cost center: ML-PLAT).
- Timeline: Need pilot access by 2026-04-01 to support labeling sprints starting Week 1 April.
- Security requirements: SOC2 Type II or equivalent evidence, vendor risk questionnaire, Data Processing Agreement (DPA), encryption at rest, and support for Okta SSO + SCIM provisioning. No PHI expected; data will include ML training examples and internal pseudonymized user content.
- Operational items: provisioning via SCIM, role mappings (editor/manager), audit log forwarding (if available), and retention policy alignment with Redwood retention standards.
- Evaluation criteria: security checklist pass, successful SCIM provisioning in staging, labeler throughput metrics (annotations/user/hour), and cost per label estimates compared to current ad-hoc tooling.
- Acceptance criteria: Security completes vendor assessment and marks as approved OR provides a list of mitigating controls; Procurement executes pilot contract; Identity provisions SCIM + Okta SSO; Finance confirms budget allocation.
- Attachments / links: Confluence vendor comparison + quote (see related_confluence_pages).

Steps to onboard / proposed playbook:
1) Procurement: confirm vendor quote, negotiate pilot term (3 months trial or credit), and draft PO.
2) Security: run vendor questionnaire (ask for SOC2 report, pen-test results, any subprocessor list).
3) Identity: validate SCIM endpoints and create test Okta app in staging.
4) Ops/ML: prepare sample dataset, test import/export, and verify retention controls.
5) Finance: pre-approve $36k headroom and route cost center charge.
6) Pilot kickoff: create 10 pilot seats, evaluate for 4 weeks, then expand to full 30 seats if OK.

Notes / risks:
- If LabelWave cannot provide SOC2 evidence, Security may require extra contractual safeguards or deny vendor onboarding.
- SCIM gaps will add manual provisioning overhead and may push timeline beyond target.
- Contractual minimums could negate the value of a small pilot; negotiate a flexible pilot credit.

Relevant historical/context links:
- See INT-142506 for precedent onboarding Doccano (similar annotation procurement): https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INT-142506

title: Okta SSO login triggers captive-portal detection which drops VPN and causes authentication loop for remote Wi‑Fi users
preview:
Issue summary: Several employees connecting from customer sites or hotel Wi‑Fi report that attempting to authenticate to internal apps via Okta SAML redirects triggers a captive‑portal flow. When users complete captive‑portal sign‑in, the VPN client (WireGuard-based corporate agent) detects the network change and tears down the tunnel, which invalidates the SSO session and returns the user to the captive portal — creating a loop.

Impact: Multiple remote employees (mostly traveling sales and solutions engineers) are unable to access internal dashboards or Atlassian tools until they fully disconnect and re‑authenticate. Affected users temporarily lose access to internal services for ~10–30 minutes. No customer‑facing systems impacted; internal productivity degradation.

Environment: macOS and Windows corporate images, Redwood VPN agent v2.9.1 (WireGuard wrapper), Okta SAML SSO, typical captive portal networks (hotel, conference Wi‑Fi), region: global (most reports from APAC/EMEA travel).

Observed behavior: SSO redirect to okta.company.com triggers captive‑portal HTTP/302 to the provider portal; the VPN agent sees an interface change and initiates rekey/reenroll which tears down existing sockets. Okta session cookie becomes invalid after the rebind and the user is presented with the captive portal again.

Expected behavior: Completing captive‑portal flow should retain network connection and VPN agent should gracefully wait for interface stabilization or pause rekey until user login completes so SSO exchange can finish.

Temporary workaround: Users must disconnect VPN, complete captive portal authentication, then re‑start VPN and re‑login to Okta. Recommended to perform captive‑portal sign‑in in a browser before starting VPN where possible.

title: Device-trust approval delay triggers repeated console access-request loop
preview:
Issue summary:
A subset of internal users attempting to sign in to the Redwood Console are stuck in an access-request loop: the console shows "Waiting for device approval" and the platform repeatedly creates new pending access-request records instead of updating the existing request. Approvers are not receiving stable notifications, and on-call pages are being generated for duplicate requests.

Impact:
- ~12 engineers across two squads reported inability to complete console login via SSO (device approval required).
- Flood of duplicate access-request tickets in the admin queue causing approver overload and delayed approvals.
- Temporary manual approvals required for critical oncall responders. No production compute or model serving impact observed.

Environment:
- Prod sign-in flow through Okta + Device Trust + internal SSO bridge (sso-bridge). Region: us-west. Affected users appear to be on iOS-managed devices and a few macOS devices with the latest MDM profile.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. User attempts console sign-in via Okta SSO from managed device.
2. Okta returns device-challenge required -> device-trust-service triggers an approval request to the approver queue.
3. The user approves on-device; device-trust service signals success, but sso-bridge polls the approval endpoint and receives intermittent 202 / empty payload due to a race with the approval indexer.
4. sso-bridge treats the response as "no approved record" and re-creates a new access-request entry (new request_id), producing duplicate pending items.
5. Approver webhooks and email notifications are rate-limited and deduplicated imperfectly, so notifications are inconsistent.

Observed logs (snippets):
- sso-bridge.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:03Z] warn: approval-poll no-record for device-req token=abc123 polling=3"
- device-trust-service.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:02Z] info: approval indexer wrote request_id=R-20260311-7"
- auth-proxy.log: "[2026-03-11T14:22:04Z] error: duplicate-access-request created new id R-20260311-8 for user=j.lee@example.com"

Hypothesis:
- A race condition between the approval indexer and the sso-bridge polling leads to a transient 202/no-body response that sso-bridge treats as "not approved" and creates a new request instead of waiting/backing off.
- Notification/dedup logic in admin-queue assumes stable request IDs and does not correlate repeated requests for the same user/device.

Immediate mitigation (already applied):
- Manual approval of critical oncall requests by SRE to restore login access.
- Temporarily increased visibility: added a dashboard query to show access-request creation rate and duplicate_count per user.

Proposed fixes / next steps:
- Short-term (hotfix): Update sso-bridge polling to interpret 202/no-body as a retryable transient and implement exponential backoff + idempotency key lookup before creating a new request. (PR planned).
- Medium-term: Harden approval indexer to emit a pre-write lease or a transient marker so pollers can detect in-progress writes.
- Long-term: Improve admin notification deduplication by matching on user+device fingerprint and recent requests (rolling window).

Attachments / references:
- Slack thread: #support (https://redwood.slack.com/archives/C0123456)
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/OPS/Device+Trust+Runbook

title: Session linkage token mismatch blocks cross-account impersonation for support tooling
preview:
Issue summary: Since 2026-03-12 09:10 UTC, support tooling (impersonation flows used by oncall engineers via the internal console and CLI) intermittently fail with a 'session token mismatch' error when initiating cross-account impersonation. Impact: Oncall engineers cannot assume customer-facing service accounts for 5-30% of impersonation attempts, blocking urgent runbook actions and escalations. Environment: production us-east, identity-proxy v3.4.1, okta-device-trust enabled for privileged flows.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Login to console as admin (device trust approved)
2) Open 'Switch account / Impersonate' dialog and select external-tenant service-account
3) Click 'Assume role', or run 'rwd auth impersonate --account <tenant-id>' via CLI
4) Observe immediate failure in UI or CLI with error code 403/SESSION_LINK_MISMATCH and short correlation id in logs

Observed behavior: The identity-proxy returns 403 with message 'session_linkage_token invalid for target session'. The console shows a transient toast 'Unable to start impersonation — session verification failed'. CLI returns non-zero exit and logs: 'auth: session_linkage: verification failed: token not in session map'.

Expected behavior: Token exchange should validate and produce a scoped session for the target account or fall back to a clear diagnostic flow requesting reauthorization.

Investigation notes:
- Correlation IDs collected from affected attempts: rwd-imp-3f9b, rwd-imp-3fa0, rwd-cli-4b12. Logs attached to ticket in the support bucket (s3://redwood-support/logs/impersonation/2026-03-12/).
- Identity-proxy logs show the incoming assertion includes a linkage_token header (base64 string). The session-store (redis cluster auth-sessions-prod) lookup for the linkage token intermittently returns a 5xx from the replica nodes leading to token not found. The failure window aligns with a 2-minute rolling restart of redis-read replicas (maintenance job kicked off at 09:09 UTC by infra auto-heal).
- Reproduced locally by toggling a TTL skew: if session-store read misses then the verification fails immediately with the same error.
- No Okta assertion failures observed; device-trust asserts ok.
- Related code paths: identity-proxy handler verifySessionLinkage(), session-cache middleware, rwd-auth-cli token exchange.

Temporary mitigation:
- Oncall instructed to retry impersonation (most retries succeed). A short-term runbook added to the support-confluence page.
- Infra team paused the rolling restart and warmed read replicas; observed reduction in failures.

Next steps / Proposed fix:
1) Add a robust retry layer to session-store reads in identity-proxy (backoff + failover to primary) — PR proposed: https://github.com/redwood-inference/identity-proxy/pull/1872
2) Improve observability: emit metrics for session-linkage cache misses and 5xx on redis replicas. Dashboard ticket created under MON-impersonation-calls.
3) Add defensive behavior: if linkage token not found but assertion from Okta valid, present clear UI guidance to reauthorize rather than generic 403.

References: Slack thread #support-impersonation (https://slack.redwoodinference/support-impersonation/p12345), PR above, redis maintenance logs (infra ticket INT-931200).

title: Okta MFA enrollment loop prevents SSO access to internal Console for new hires
preview:
Issue summary:
Several new hires cannot complete Okta SSO to access the Redwood Console because they enter an MFA enrollment loop. The Okta flow repeatedly prompts for MFA enrollment and then immediately returns to the login screen without granting a session. This affects onboarding engineers and blocks critical access to runbooks and incident tooling.

Impact:
- 8 new employees (onboarding cohort) unable to access console since 2026-03-12 08:20 UTC.
- Prevents completion of onboarding tasks and emergency incident response for affected users.

Environment:
- Redwood Console (https://console.redwood.ai) behind Okta SSO.
- Affected in prod/us-east for new users created through SCIM this morning.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create test user via SCIM with same group set as onboarding cohort.
2) Attempt first-time SSO login at https://console.redwood.ai via Okta.
3) Okta prompts for MFA enrollment (SMS / Authenticator).
4) After selecting a factor and completing enrollment flow, Okta redirects back to login and immediately prompts for MFA enrollment again (loop).

Observed behavior / logs:
- Okta auth logs (excerpt):
  - user.login.attempt produced eventId=0oab1234xyz; outcome=FAILURE; reason=FACTOR_ENROLLMENT_INCOMPLETE
  - factor.enroll.attempt eventId=0ocd5678abc; outcome=SUCCESS; enrollmentState=ENROLLMENT_BLOCKED
- Browser network: repeated POST /idp/idx/interaction/2003 responses returning remediation requiring enroll factor again.
- Console shows 401 for session validation when Okta returns session token.

First response SLA due: 2026-03-12

title: Service accounts lost Console/CI access after Okta group prefix remapping
preview: Issue summary: Several internal service accounts and a small set of CI pipelines started receiving 403/permission-denied errors when calling the Redwood Console API and internal admin endpoints immediately after an Okta group naming convention change was applied by Identity. Impact: 7 service accounts and 4 CI workflows failed auth checks; internal tooling and alerting UIs were inaccessible for owners. Environment: Production identity sync (SCIM) + Redwood Console RBAC mapping, us-east region. Timeline: group prefix remapping applied 2026-03-10 07:15 UTC, first failures observed 07:21 UTC. Expected: group membership mapping should preserve effective RBAC roles for service principals and bot users. Observed: group-based roles no longer resolved for SAML/SCIM-provisioned service accounts because Okta attribute used for mapping changed from groups.name to groups.displayName in the recent change.

title: Workstation experiencing thermal shutdowns and 0 RPM fan telemetry after BIOS firmware rollout
preview:
Issue summary:
Several corporate developer laptops started reporting fan RPM as 0 in telemetry and have experienced thermal throttling or unexpected shutdowns after the March 8th BIOS firmware push. Affected devices are primarily Lenovo ThinkPad fleet that received the v1.2.3 BIOS rollback/upgrade during nightly autopilot/intune maintenance window.

Impact:
- Multiple engineers reported sudden shutdowns while compiling or running local model-serving containers.
- Telemetry shows CPU package temps > 95C and fan RPM 0 prior to shutdown.
- A subset of devices are recoverable by power-cycling; others require manual reseating of fan cable or BIOS re-flash.

Environment:
- corp-laptop | us-west region endpoints registered in Intune
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 22H2 with latest cumulative updates
- Endpoint management: Intune + Autopilot + custom vendor BIOS automation job

title: New-hire onboarding: data-visualization console access stuck due to identity crosswalk mapping mismatch
preview: Issue summary: A recently hired PM cannot access the internal DataViz console after completing SSO and SCIM provisioning. The user record appears in Okta and SCIM logs, but the console returns 403 with a message 'no roles assigned'. Impact: New hire onboarding blocked (cannot run dashboards or join pairing session). This is delaying a planned sync meeting tomorrow with the product team and is time-sensitive. Environment: Production (us-west), internal console behind identity-proxy, SCIM ingest pipeline v2.2.2. Steps to reproduce: 1) Invite new user via HR-provisioner -> Okta created profile. 2) Auto-provisioner runs SCIM PATCH to identity service. 3) User signs in via Okta SSO and is authenticated, but the console shows 403 and the support-runbook 'check-user-roles' returns empty role list. Observed logs: SCIM PATCH succeeded (200). Okta event shows user assigned to group "prod-dataviz-users". Identity-proxy log shows upstream call to authz service returned 204 NoContent. The console auth middleware logs: "role_lookup: 0 roles for subject=okta|12345-abcd". Hypothesis: crosswalk mapping between Okta group displayName and internal role key is failing due to a legacy transform that strips punctuation and lowercases; some groups created by IT include a trailing non-ASCII hyphen which is not normalized and therefore fails match in the role catalog.

title: Runner secret-cache livelock during bulk key rewrap blocks release cutover
preview:
Issue summary:
During a scheduled bulk KMS rewrap for ephemeral runner credentials, a subset of runners entered a secret-cache livelock where vault-sync repeatedly refreshed secrets but the runner-provisioner refused to mark nodes healthy. This prevented the release cutover job from acquiring the required pool of labeled runners and blocked the production deploy window.

Impact:
- Release cutover delayed for two release pipelines (nightly + hotfix).
- ~18% of ephemeral runner fleet in us-west-2 and 12% in eu-central-1 continuously flapped between REGISTERED and UNHEALTHY, failing job assignment.
- Some long-running jobs were evicted and re-queued, creating billing churn.

Environment:
- prod account (redwood-prod) running 2026-03-10 KMS rewrap job initiated from operations-rotate-key pipeline.
- Runner image v2026.02.1, runner-provisioner v1.14.0, vault-sync v0.9.3.
- Autoscaler policy: conservative-surge with max-unavailable 10%.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Triggered bulk KMS rewrap for ephemeral runner service account keys across regions.
2) vault-sync pushed new wrapped secrets and incremented secret-version labels on k8s Secret objects.
3) Runner pods attempted to fetch new secret; cache layer (runner-provisioner cache) attempted a soft-refresh but observed nonce mismatch with authz-cache table.
4) Provisioner flagged pod as unhealthy and restarted the token renewal flow. vault-sync simultaneously rewrapped again due to perceived failure, renewing secret-version and causing the runner to see a new version mid-refresh.
5) Loop repeated until operator intervention.

Logs / Observations:
- Vault-sync logs (us-west-2): many 2026-03-10T22:14:xx entries "secret rewrap: success version=222->223" followed quickly by retries for same pod IDs.
- Runner-provisioner logs: repeated "cache nonce mismatch, dropping token refresh" followed by "marking runner UNHEALTHY: token-refresh-failed" for same container IDs.
- Authz-cache (redis) had transient high CPU and eviction spikes coincident with the rewrap window.
- Scheduler metrics showed job-assign latency spike to 4.1s (baseline 0.6s) and pending-job queue growth.

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis: concurrent rewraps + eventual consistency of authz-cache caused the provisioner to observe mixed secret-versions across components and treat that as compromise, triggering aggressive eviction.
- Vault-sync retry/backoff is too eager when secrets appear outdated; it will rewrap again for a pod perceived as failed, which escalates version churn.
- The provisioner cache validates both secret-version and nonce; if versions do not align within a short window, it refuses to accept the token and evicts. The short acceptance window is too tight for our multi-region rewrap choreography.
- Related: similar pattern to INT-30542 (runner deregistered during credentials rotation) but here it manifests as livelock due to compounded rewrap retries across components.

Temporary workaround implemented:
- Paused the rolling rewrap in eu-central-1 and set vault-sync to single-threaded mode for us-west-2.
- Increased provisioner token acceptance window (config toggle) from 5s to 45s as an emergency mitigation to allow reconciliation.
- Manually reconciled ~120 affected pods by forcing a single rewrap and restarting runner-provisioner for the affected node pool.

Resolution plan (short-term):
- Stabilize by rolling a hotfix to vault-sync to add optimistic backoff and a per-pod rewrap gate so we don't rewrap the same pod while a previous refresh is in-flight. Target: vault-sync v0.9.4.
- Roll hotfix to runner-provisioner to make cache-version acceptance window configurable and to add a one-time idempotency token check to tolerate brief version skew. Target: v1.14.1.
- Increase authz-cache resource requests and add Redis persistence tuning to avoid eviction during rotation windows.
- Re-run controlled rotation with feature flags enabling single-region stagger and observability hooks.

Follow-ups and postmortem items:
- Postmortem to be authored once stable with timeline, blame-free root cause, and remediation owner assignments.
- Add automated test that simulates concurrent rewrap + cache skew to CI test harness.
- Update the Release Cutover Checklist to include a secret-rotation readiness gate.
- Consider orchestration change: central coordinator that stages rewraps per runner label instead of region-wide parallel rewraps.

title: Okta claim encoding collision prevents RBAC provisioning for internal ops console
preview:
Issue summary:
Users are denied access to the Ops Console with a 403 RBAC error even though their Okta profiles include the expected groups. The denial appears tied to group claim encoding changes introduced by last week's Okta connector upgrade (v2.7.1).

Impact:
Multiple engineering and SRE users cannot access runbook/incident tooling. This blocks oncall rotations and delays incident response. Affected users are primarily in us-west and include contractors and full-time staff.

Observed behavior:
- Users report 403 when opening /console/runbooks (UI) and 403 from CLI tooling that hits the same token-exchange endpoint.
- Okta shows group membership correctly (group names include em-dash and accented characters for some internal teams).
- Our identity proxy logs show group claim payloads with mixed percent-encoding and UTF-8 bytes, which the RBAC service fails to normalize.

Expected behavior:
Group claims should be normalized to our internal canonical format and RBAC grants applied, so users with the correct groups receive the expected console roles.

Steps to reproduce (observed with affected user):
1) User signs in via Okta SSO and hits /auth/callback.
2) Identity proxy receives id_token with claim groups: [\"eng—platform\", \"oncall-équipe\"] where the em-dash and accented e are present.
3) After connector upgrade, some tokens contain percent-encoded bytes like \"eng%EF%80%81-platform\" or a mix of bytes and Unicode characters.
4) RBAC mapping stage performs a literal string lookup against canonical group keys and does not match, resulting in a 403.

Relevant logs and artifacts:
- Identity proxy request sample (redacted) available in link: slack://channel/#support?thread=G12345 (private logs pinned).
- Auth service logs: auth-proxy@us-west-02:2026-03-10T09:12:23Z - WARN group_claim_parse failed for user u-8743 (payload contains invalid percent-encoding).
- GitHub issue from connector vendor: https://github.com/okta/okta-connector/issues/4183 (related encoding change).
- Confluence runbook draft: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPS/okta-claim-normalization (internal).

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis 1: The v2.7.1 connector changed how multi-byte characters are serialized into group claims, moving from pure UTF-8 to a percent-encoded fallback for certain legacy Okta org profiles.
- Hypothesis 2: The identity proxy previously accepted both percent-encoded and UTF-8 forms and normalized them; a recent auth-proxy upgrade (2026-03-08) added stricter validation that rejects mixed encodings.
- Quick test: Reproduced by creating test user with group name containing em-dash (—) and forced connector to return percent-encoded sequence; identity proxy returned a parsing error and RBAC lookup failed.
- Affected surface: UI auth flow, CLI token-exchange, and service-to-service token refresh where group claims are evaluated.

Mitigation attempted so far:
- Rolled back auth-proxy change on one canary host to re-enable tolerant parsing; that host successfully allowed previously blocked user to authenticate and receive roles.
- Workaround for affected users: Use the legacy session endpoint (session-legacy.redwood.ai) which hits the older proxy path that still normalizes claims. This is not sustainable.

Next steps / proposed fix:
1) Short-term: Patch auth-proxy to implement a normalization pass that percent-decodes group claim strings then applies Unicode NFC normalization before RBAC lookup. Target ETA: 2026-03-13 EOD for hotfix.
2) Medium-term: Add automated unit tests and fuzzing for claim encodings in identity-proxy repo.
3) Long-term: Coordinate with Okta connector maintainers to ensure consistent serialization and add integration test coverage.

Related items:
- linked_issues: [\"INT-202999\", \"INT-4251\"]
  (INT-202999 is an earlier Okta claim mismatch ticket; INT-4251 references group sync dashboard access issue.)

Resolution notes (planned):
- Hotfix auth-proxy on fleet will be rolled out via canary -> 25% -> 100% with monitoring on auth-errors and RBAC-deny rates.
- Post-deploy, ask impacted users to re-login and confirm access.

title: Routing escalation matrix drift affecting SLA-based routing for multi-tenant support flows
preview:
Issue summary:
Support tooling routing rules have diverged from the canonical SLA escalation matrix for a subset of multi-tenant routes. Some P1 routes for Dedicated-tier customers are being escalated to L2 on the wrong schedule (30m instead of 15m), and a few self-serve routes are hitting manual-intervention macros unexpectedly.

Impact:
- Potential customer-visible violation of escalation SLA for ~4 Dedicated customers over the last 48h.
- Increased mean time to engage for urgent tickets; reported by on-call as customer-impacting but intermittent.
- Reporting shows mismatched counts between routing-engine logs and support-console SLA buckets.

Scope / affected areas:
- Routing-engine rule set for multi-tenant routes generated by sla-config-service v1.9.1
- Support console macros for escalation (templates/escalate/*)
- Reporting pipeline aggregations for SLA breach counts

Initial hypothesis:
- A config templating regression in sla-config-service produced stale escalation intervals for routes created via the bulk-importer job run 2026-03-08.
- A subsequent partial rollback of config templates may have left a hybrid state: some routes referencing legacy policy IDs while the console expects the new policy schema.

Steps to reproduce (investigator notes):
1) Query routing-engine for routes created between 2026-03-07 and 2026-03-09 with tag:multi-tenant=true and customer_tier=dedicated.
2) Compare escalation.interval and escalation.policy_id between returned entries and SLA canonical table from Confluence.
3) Trigger a synthetic ticket against a sample route and observe escalation timing via support-console metrics.

Observed logs / telemetry pointers:
- routing-engine/error.log contains repeated WARN: "policy_lookup_fallback: policy_id 0x1f not found, using legacy_timing" (2026-03-09 03:12:07 UTC).
- sla-config-service audit trail shows bulk-import job id BI-20260308 completed with 1,246 updated routes.
- support-console and reporting-pipeline counts diverge by ~5-8% for P1 escalation events in us-east for 2026-03-09.

Immediate impact mitigation (temporary):
- Support on-call asked to manually reassign affected tickets to L2 if escalation timing appears delayed.
- Rolled forward a guard rule in routing-engine to enforce minimum escalation interval of 15m for any route with tier=dedicated (hotfix applied 2026-03-10 05:40 UTC).

Requested next steps:
- Full diff of sla-config templates from v1.9.0 -> v1.9.1 and the bulk-importer transformations.
- Re-run the bulk-import job in dry-run against a staging snapshot and validate policy_id mapping logic.
- Reconcile reporting counts after authoritative fix and backfill any missed escalations if needed.
- Postmortem and a small automation: validation job that flags routes where escalation.interval != canonical_interval.

title: Machine cert CRL/OCSP fetch failure causes VPN tunnel collapse and loss of internal DNS suffixes
preview:
Issue summary: After a scheduled corporate machine certificate rotation, a subset of macOS and Windows hosts fail to establish the zero-trust VPN agent. Impact: Affected hosts lose corporate DNS suffix resolution (internal.*), causing service access failures (k8s API, internal dashboards, git over https). Scope: ~120 hosts in us-east over the last 24h. Pattern: failures cluster around clients that were asleep or on flaky Wi-Fi during cert push.

Observed behavior: Zero-trust agent shows cert validation errors; system logs show CRL/OCSP fetch timeouts; VPN client repeatedly tears down and rebinds network interfaces which clears DNS suffixes. Expected behavior: Agent should accept new machine certs or gracefully retry CRL/OCSP without dropping tunnel/DNS on transient fetch failures.

Customer/Business impact: Internal developer productivity affected; CI jobs hitting internal artifact registries failing; small number of oncall SREs unable to access internal consoles.

preview: Issue summary: A supplier invoice submitted via EDI (X12 810) with three invoice lines was posted in the finance ledger with no cost-center/project attributes on any lines; the invoice header defaulted to Project 0000. The invoice was accepted by the EDI ingestion service but the AP posting in the ERP dropped per-line cost-center tags when the invoice contained 3+ lines that reference PO line splits.

title: Provisioning stall after depot repair: kernel module signature enforcement blocks corporate NIC driver and prevents management check-in
preview: Issue summary: Several Dell Latitude 7440 units returned from depot repair fail to fully provision after the post-repair image. The corporate NIC kernel module (corp-nic.ko) is present on disk but is not loaded due to kernel module signature enforcement. Because the signed driver is rejected, the system falls back to a generic NIC, which receives a DHCP address on an isolated vlan and cannot reach SCCM/Intune, causing provisioning/check-in to stall. Impact: devices remain in 'staged' state, cannot retrieve device policies or complete post-image configuration, delaying reprovisioning and increasing depot turnover time.

title: Provision ephemeral IAM broker role and cross-account access for runtime debugging across GCP/AWS
preview:
Issue summary:
We need a short-lived, auditable broker role that lets SRE/Platform engineers assume cross-account access into customer-facing Dedicated and Private environments (AWS/GCP) for live debugging and incident response. The broker should centralize approval, rotate credentials automatically, and emit audit events to the central logging pipeline.

Background:
Recent incidents required manual cross-account key exchange and one-off service-account impersonation. That flow caused delays and missing audit context when engineers debugged GPU-backed inference stacks in customer staging and prod. We want a managed ephemeral path that enforces least privilege and automatic expiry.

Impact:
- Time-to-diagnose is increased for Sev1/Sev2 incidents affecting dedicated/private customers.
- Manual processes create orphaned access snapshots and inconsistent audit trails.

Request / Acceptance criteria:
- Provision a broker IAM role (AWS) and a service-account impersonation flow (GCP) that can be assumed via a short-lived session (max 1 hour).
- Central approval: integrate with internal approval workflow (Slack + Approvals API) and capture approver identity in audit logs.
- RBAC mapping: broker maps to scoped roles (e.g., readonly-serving, gpu-debug) rather than full admin.
- Automatic credential lifecycle: no long-lived keys; sessions are ephemeral and recorded.
- Logging: all assume/impersonation events logged to central Cloud Logging and forwarded to SIEM.
- Revocation: ability to immediately revoke active sessions and list active sessions per engineer.

Security constraints and notes:
- No direct customer secrets or keys are exposed in Slack.
- Use KMS/HSM-backed signing for temporary credentials issuance where supported.
- All changes must be reviewed by Security before prod enablement.

Expiry policy and audit retention:
- Default session TTL: 30 minutes; configurable up to 1 hour with justification.
- Audit records retained according to compliance policy (90 days hot, 1 year cold).

Owners and timeline:
- Owner: Diego Morales (Platform/SRE)

title: SSO auth proxy header collision causes intermittent 401/403 for analytics gateway login
preview:
Issue summary:
Users and SREs reported intermittent 401/403 when logging into the analytics portal and when machine agents call the insights API from app-hosted flows. Errors began appearing after proxy middleware v2.14.0 was deployed to prod on 2026-03-09.

Impact:
- ~8% of analytics-portal login attempts fail during peak minutes (observed 09:00–11:00 PDT).
- Automated ingestion jobs that rely on service-to-service SSO occasionally fail with 401s, triggering retries and delayed dashboards.

Environment:
- Prod (us-west-2) behind identity-proxy fleet (k8s, istio sidecar + custom auth middleware).

Observed behavior:
- Requests that should be allowed by Okta SSO show a 401 from the analytics-gateway.
- When retries succeed, request headers show slightly different casing/order for auth-related headers.
- No broad Okta outage; only services routed through the updated proxy are affected.

First seen: 2026-03-09 08:54 PDT (alerts from support + SRE dashboards).
