title: Intermittent high latency and packet loss for staff connected to corporate VPN over office Wi-Fi — suspected MTU/fragmentation
preview: Issue summary: Over the last 48 hours several engineers on the us-west office Wi‑Fi (Corp-STA) reported intermittent high latency (200–800ms) and packet loss when accessing internal services hosted in our VPC while connected to the corporate IPSec VPN. Impact: Developer workflow degraded (git pushes, large artifact downloads time out), remote debugging sessions dropped. No impact reported for staff on wired desks or staff using the Dedicated VLAN SSID. Environment: Office Wi‑Fi (Corp-STA SSID) on AireOS AP controller (firmware 8.10.140.0), IPSec gateway (vendor: FortiGate FG-300E) with UDP encapsulation (NAT-T), client VPN is our standard AnyConnect replacement (custom), client OSes affected: Windows 10/11 and some macOS 12 clients. Steps to reproduce: 1) Connect to Corp-STA Wi‑Fi in us-west office. 2) Establish VPN to corporate gateway. 3) Run: ping -s 1400 10.0.5.10 (internal service) or scp a ~50MB artifact from internal repo. 4) Observe latency spikes, occasional 'Destination Unreachable (Fragmentation needed)'. Observed behaviour: Large TCP transfers stall or timeout, ICMP 'fragmentation needed' seen originating from gateway/firewall in packet captures; traceroute shows stable path until AP controller/handoff point then hops spike. Expected behaviour: Stable sub-50ms latency with no packet loss over Wi‑Fi when connected to VPN. Attachments: packet_capture_corrupted.pcap (sensitive), traceroute_logs.txt, AP-controller-config-snippet.txt

title: Post-SSD repair: systemd-resume hang after hibernation prevents fleet enrollment and SCCM check-in on Ubuntu 22.04
preview:
Issue summary:
After SSD replacement and standard depot provisioning, several Ubuntu 22.04 laptops are failing to complete post-repair check-in. The devices appear to boot normally but never reach fleet enrollment (fleet-agent) and SCCM client check-in. Investigations show the machine is stuck in the systemd 'resume' target after a hibernation/resume cycle triggered by the depot hardware test.

Impact:
- Affected devices are not joining inventory or receiving policy, delaying redeployment to users.
- ~6 devices reported from depot on 2026-03-08 and 2026-03-09.
- Operational impact: rework and manual reprovisioning required for redeployments.

Environment:
- Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (custom corporate image built via imaging pipeline)
- kernel 5.15.0-80-generic (corp-pinned)
- Fleet-agent v1.8.2, SCCM agent shim for Linux v2.1.0
- SSD vendor: Western Digital SN730 (replacement units from depot pool)
- Boot mode: UEFI, Secure Boot enabled

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Depot engineer replaces NVMe SSD with stock SN730 unit and re-flashes corporate image via imaging station (PXE → task sequence).
2) Imaging completes and system runs hardware self-test which forces a suspend/hibernate cycle at end of task sequence (this is new in depot script rev 2026-02-28).
3) Machine resumes and reaches systemd resume.target but several services never transition to running (fleet-agent, sccm-shim).
4) Device appears responsive over serial/console, but fleet enroll never completes and SCCM check-in does not occur.

Observed logs / artifacts:
- journalctl -b shows repeated timeouts in systemd-resume.service and a backtrace for kernel resume_resume_device for NVMe (attached lines below).
- /var/log/fleet-agent.log ends with \"waiting for network\" but networking shows UP from ip link.
- dmesg shows \"nvme 0000:01:00.0: failed to resume queue 0\" intermittently.

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis 1: NVMe driver resume path regression when using replacement SN730 units + current kernel interacting with corporate init ordering.
- Hypothesis 2: the depot's testing script forces a hibernate state that trips a systemd ordering edge case where resume.target occurs before network.target, causing fleet-agent's registration to wait indefinitely behind a masked wait.
- Reproduced on one lab device by explicitly triggering hibernate after imaging; same symptoms observed.
- Booting with \"noresume\" kernel param allows normal boot and fleet enrollment proceeds, indicating resume code path is implicated.
- Kernel upgrade test candidate (5.15.0-90) in staging reduced resume hang frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Vendor NVMe firmware on affected replacement units is 1.0.3; depot stock contains mixed firmware versions.

Temporary workaround:

Issue summary:
After SSD replacement and standard depot provisioning, several Ubuntu 22.04 laptops are failing to complete post-repair check-in. The devices appear to boot normally but never reach fleet enrollment (fleet-agent) and SCCM client check-in. Investigations show the machine is stuck in the systemd 'resume' target after a hibernation/resume cycle triggered by the depot hardware test.

Impact:
- Affected devices are not joining inventory or receiving policy, delaying redeployment to users.
- ~6 devices reported from depot on 2026-03-08 and 2026-03-09.
- Operational impact: rework and manual reprovisioning required for redeployments.

Environment:
- Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (custom corporate image built via imaging pipeline)
- kernel 5.15.0-80-generic (corp-pinned)
- Fleet-agent v1.8.2, SCCM agent shim for Linux v2.1.0
- SSD vendor: Western Digital SN730 (replacement units from depot pool)
- Boot mode: UEFI, Secure Boot enabled

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Depot engineer replaces NVMe SSD with stock SN730 unit and re-flashes corporate image via imaging station (PXE → task sequence).
2) Imaging completes and system runs hardware self-test which forces a suspend/hibernate cycle at end of task sequence (this is new in depot script rev 2026-02-28).
3) Machine resumes and reaches systemd resume.target but several services never transition to running (fleet-agent, sccm-shim).
4) Device appears responsive over serial/console, but fleet enroll never completes and SCCM check-in does not occur.

Observed logs / artifacts:
- journalctl -b shows repeated timeouts in systemd-resume.service and a backtrace for kernel resume_resume_device for NVMe (attached lines below).
- /var/log/fleet-agent.log ends with \"waiting for network\" but networking shows UP from ip link.
- dmesg shows \"nvme 0000:01:00.0: failed to resume queue 0\" intermittently.

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis 1: NVMe driver resume path regression when using replacement SN730 units + current kernel interacting with corporate init ordering.
- Hypothesis 2: the depot's testing script forces a hibernate state that trips a systemd ordering edge case where resume.target occurs before network.target, causing fleet-agent's registration to wait indefinitely behind a masked wait.
- Reproduced on one lab device by explicitly triggering hibernate after imaging; same symptoms observed.
- Booting with \"noresume\" kernel param allows normal boot and fleet enrollment proceeds, indicating resume code path is implicated.
- Kernel upgrade test candidate (5.15.0-90) in staging reduced resume hang frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Vendor NVMe firmware on affected replacement units is 1.0.3; depot stock contains mixed firmware versions.

Temporary workaround:
- Depot engineers should avoid triggering the hibernate step in the post-imaging script (reverted to previous script version 2026-02-21) until a fix is validated.
- For devices already affected: boot with kernel parameter \"noresume\" then re-trigger normal boot to allow fleet enrollment; technicians can then re-image if desired.

- Imaging team to roll back depot script to pre-hibernate version (owner: Priya Shah, due 2026-03-12).
- Kernel team to validate whether kernel resume path fixes in 5.15.0-90/5.19 backports resolve the NVMe resume failures (owner: Ethan Park, in progress).
- Procurement/dev-ops to audit depot NVMe firmware and segregate SN730 units with firmware 1.0.3 (owner: Marco Li, due 2026-03-15).
- Create focused test matrix: SSD vendor/firmware x kernel version x hibernate enabled/disabled. Capture reproducibility matrix and logs (owner: Imaging QA).

Acceptance criteria for resolution:
- Depot imaging script can run with hibernate test and devices consistently resume and complete fleet enrollment without manual intervention.
- Root cause documented and either kernel patch, firmware update, or imaging change implemented.

title: NetworkManager writes duplicate default routes during imaging causing AAD join timeout in ap-southeast
preview:
Issue summary:
During automated task-sequence imaging in APAC (ap-southeast-1), NetworkManager on fresh Windows PE handoff writes two overlapping default routes (0.0.0.0/0) to the primary interface. The duplicated routes have different metrics and intermittently cause the Azure AD join step to time out (AADSTS network error) or attempt to use an internal management VPN endpoint, failing domain registration and stalling the task sequence.

Impact:
- ~5-8% of devices in the last 48h fail AAD join during imaging and are left in the 'Autopilot not completed' state.
- Affected devices require manual intervention (local admin join + re-run of post-provision scripts) delaying depot turn-around.
- Mostly impacts APAC staging fleet and a small subset of production depot images.

Environment:
- Imaging orchestrator: SCCM task sequence handing off to WinPE -> OOBE scripts (internal imaging-playbooks v3.7).
- Network manager: Custom NM shim used in WinPE handoff (win-nm-wrapper v0.9.2).
- Observed on Lenovo ThinkPad X1 Carbon Gen11, Dell Latitude 7440.
- Regions: ap-southeast-1 staging and select prod depots.

Steps to reproduce (intermittent):
1) Boot device into SCCM task sequence using depot network VLAN (APAC depot).
2) WinPE hands off to windows setup, imaging-playbook triggers win-nm-wrapper to apply DHCP/route config.
3) Watch netstat / route print or use PowerShell Get-NetRoute after handoff.
4) Observe two default routes: one with metric 15 and one with metric 5, sometimes bound to same interface.
5) Continue imaging -> Azure AD Join step times out or reports network unreachable.

Observed behavior:
- Duplicate default routes appear immediately after win-nm-wrapper runs; sometimes the lower-metric route points at a management VLAN gateway that should be unreachable from the depot VLAN.
- AAD join logs show network requests routed to the wrong gateway and either timing out or returning HTTP 502 from an internal proxy.
- Re-running networking script that deletes the duplicate route allows AAD join to succeed.

Expected behavior:
- Single default route to depot gateway (correct metric).
- AAD join uses depot network path to Microsoft endpoints.

Logs / Artifacts attached in ticket: list of sample Get-NetRoute outputs, win-nm-wrapper logs, SCCM task sequence trace snippets, sample AAD join traces (correlation IDs) and pcap from one repro.

Investigation notes:
- Initial hypothesis: race between DHCP client registering routes and win-nm-wrapper adding explicit route entries leads to duplicate entries.
- win-nm-wrapper v0.9.2 introduced a change to add a default-route fallback with lower metric when DHCP lease is delayed (PR infra/imaging-playbooks#157). Suspect this path improperly fires even when DHCP later populates the route.
- Correlated failures with depot switches that have intermittent DHCP lease latency (~100-400ms) during peak provisioning windows.
- Reproduced locally by delaying DHCP response in a lab: when wrapper adds fallback route and DHCP later adds its route, system ends up with two default routes. Windows route selection uses metric so traffic can go to unintended gateway.

Temporary workaround:
- Add a cleanup step in task sequence immediately after win-nm-wrapper: run PowerShell to remove duplicate default routes, keeping the DHCP-provided one (script snippet available). Deployed to staging depots, reduced failures to ~0.5% in first 12h.

Next steps / action items:
- Engineering to patch win-nm-wrapper to check for an existing default route and avoid adding fallback when DHCP is expected (owner: infra/imaging team).
- Long-term: update imaging-playbooks to wait for DHCP lease or implement an idempotent route reconcile step; add unit test for delayed DHCP scenarios.
- Ops: monitor APAC depot provisioning metrics and roll the cleanup script to remaining depots pending patch.

Resolution (work in progress):
- Temporary cleanup script deployed to 2 staging depots -- effective as a stopgap.
- Patch PR opened: infra/imaging-playbooks#157 (fix adds route-existence check and 2s wait for DHCP). Pending code review and canary.

title: Authorization drift: tag-based IAM condition prevents dedicated-provisioner from completing cross-account role exchange in prod us-east
preview:
Issue summary:
During an automated Dedicated capacity scale-up (provisioner job) in prod/us-east, the dedicated-provisioner is failing to complete the AWS->GCP cross-account role exchange step. New worker nodes are left in a provisioning loop and cannot register with the scheduling service.

Impact:
- Provisioner failures are blocking dedicated pool scaling for two customer-facing pools (internal monitoring shows ~22% reduced capacity).
- Intermittent customer API throttling for dedicated customers relying on autoscale; no data loss but degraded throughput.

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated provisioning pipeline (prod:us-east)
- Affected components: dedicated-provisioner (k8s Job), STS assume-role workflow, GCP service account impersonation to read KMS and secret manager artifacts.

Steps to reproduce (observed in prod):
1) Trigger scale-up of dedicated pool via the autoscaler.
2) Provisioner creates AWS EC2 instances and invokes STS:AssumeRole with session tags set by scheduler.
3) AssumeRole returns success but downstream IAM condition rejects subsequent GCP impersonation (role-binding audit shows DENY at policy-eval for principal tag mismatch).

Observed behavior:
- Logs: "AccessDenied: IAM condition 'request.tag:team' mismatch" then "gcp-impersonation: principal denied".
- Provisioner retries until job backoff limit and then marks node as failed.

Expected behavior:
- AssumeRole + session tags should be honored and the downstream GCP identity broker should accept the mapped principal; provisioning should complete normally.

Attachments:
- Provisioner logs (prod-us-east, last 24h) attached to ticket.
- IAM policy snapshots (AWS role trust policy and GCP IAM binding) uploaded to infra artifacts.

title: Delegated access approval hierarchy blocking console elevation for engineers after Okta group merge
preview:
Issue summary:
A delegated access request for console elevation is stuck in the approval workflow for multiple engineers after an Okta group merge. The approval UI shows 'Pending - waiting for approver' even though required approvers are listed and have approved. This prevents engineers from obtaining temporary elevated roles to access production dashboards and runbooks.

Impact:
- 6 engineers are blocked from elevating to a support role across multiple teams (SRE, Platform).
- On-call rotation had to manually escalate via Slack and temporary token sharing, increasing operational risk.
- No customer-facing outages, but internal response time for incidents increased.

Environment:
- Production (us-east)
- Affected service: approval-microservice v1.3.2, rbac-service v2.0.1, okta-scim-sync job (daily)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create a delegated access request via Console -> Access -> Request Elevation (select 'support-engineer' role).
2) Add approvers from two different teams (team leads who are members of Okta groups).
3) Confirm approvals in the Console UI. Approver shows as confirmed but request remains Pending.

Observed behavior:
- UI shows approver checkboxes checked and timestamps on approver actions, but the request state is still 'Pending' and no role assignment is created in RBAC service.
- approval-microservice logs contain repeated errors of parent-role lookup failures: "parent role id lookup returned null for role_id=role-1243"
- Okta SCIM sync shows a recent group merge event at 2026-03-09 23:05 UTC (group A merged into group B).

Expected behavior:
- After required approvers approve, the request transitions to 'Approved' and rbac-service issues the temporary role assignment with TTL for the requester.

Relevant logs and links:
- approval-microservice error sample: 
  2026-03-11T02:12:45Z ERROR approval: failed to compute approval cascade: null parent for role=role-1243 request_id=REQ-20260311-132
- Okta SCIM sync job trace: https://ci.redwood-inference.internal/okta-sync/5678
- Slack thread: https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C0ABCDEF1/p1678550400000500
- Related infra PR (applied partially): https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/4321

Temporary workaround:
- Support engineers with admin privileges performed a manual role assignment in rbac-service for affected users. This restores access but bypasses the normal approval audit trail.

Next steps planned:
1) Investigate why parent-role lookup returned null after Okta group merge. Hypothesis: role inheritance cache references an Okta group ID that was deleted during the merge and the approval-microservice does not handle a missing parent gracefully.
2) Re-run a scoped role-propagation job to rebuild role lineage cache for the merged groups.
3) Patch approval-microservice to treat missing parent references as soft-missing and surface a clearer error + auto-retry path.
4) Add monitoring/alerting on approval-failure rates and parent-lookup errors.

title: Clarify provisional license reclaim window and equipment-return grace period for immediate offboarding
preview:
Issue summary:
HR received reports from Finance and IT that when an employee is placed on Immediate Termination (ITerm) in Workday, the seat/license in third-party SaaS (notably the design/legal tool vendor) remains billable for up to 14 days and the assigned corporate laptop sometimes remains tagged as 'active' in our asset management inventory. This ticket seeks clarification of the intended policy (grace windows) and the operational mechanics (which systems enforce the reclaim/return windows).

Impact:
- Potential short-term double-billing for SaaS seats when seats are quickly re-assigned.
- Delays in device recovery leading to inventory inaccuracies and security exposure for high-risk departures.
- Confusion between HR, IT, and Finance on expected timelines and required manual steps.

Environment:
- Identity sync: Okta -> SCIM -> downstream SaaS (prod)
- Asset tracking: FleetOps (prod) with inventory sync to ITSM
- HR system: Workday (prod)

Observed behavior / Steps to reproduce:
1) Create an employee termination in Workday with 'Immediate' flag at 09:00 UTC.
2) Workday emits termination event, HR webhook updates Okta.
3) Okta SCIM provisioning sends deactivation to SaaS vendor; we observe the user status change but vendor maintains seat as billable for a 14-day grace window (vendor policy).
4) FleetOps does not mark the laptop as 'awaiting pickup' until an asset-handback task is created manually by IT; automatic tagging is not triggered by the termination event.
5) Finance reports a new invoice line for an additional seat 3 days later after license reassign because the old seat was not reclaimed yet.

Logs / evidence: 
- Okta SCIM audit (okta-audit-2026-03-09.log) shows deactivation event at 09:02 UTC and response 200 from vendor with response body: {"status":"deprovision-scheduled","grace_days":14}.
- FleetOps event stream shows no asset lifecycle change tied to the Workday termination event (fleetops-events-2026-03-09.ndjson).
- Finance ticket FIN-1189 referencing unexpected seat charge for week of 2026-03-02.

Question / Requested clarification:
- What are the company-wide defaults for SaaS license reclaim windows and how many days are considered 'standard' vs 'exception' requiring manual immediate reclaim?
- For Immediate Terminations, do we have an established exception/accelerated flow that forces vendor-side seat revocation and device pickup tagging?
- Which teams are expected to take manual actions vs which actions should be automated by identity/asset tooling?

Desired outcome:
- A concise policy statement HR/IT/Finance can rely on for immediate-term offboards, including: default reclaim window, criteria for accelerating reclaim, and the single authoritative runbook link for immediate offboards.

title: CI Terraform run blocked: CMEK/KMS keyRing and key creation denied by org-policy during dedicated-prod onboarding
preview:
Issue summary:
CI (terraform) provisioning step that creates a KMS KeyRing and CMEK key in project redwood-prod-dedicated failed with org-policy DENY on keyRing.create and serviceAccount.impersonate. This blocks onboarding of analytics-replicator-sa for Dedicated environment and prevents terraform apply from completing.

Impact:
- New Dedicated capacity onboarding is blocked (analytics-replicator service) delaying customer migration.
- CI pipeline failing on every run; engineers cannot complete infra changes requiring CMEK.
- Risk of manual key creation and out-of-band grants if not resolved quickly.

Environment:
- GCP project: redwood-prod-dedicated
- CI: github-actions self-hosted runners in prod-us-west
- Terraform version: 1.6.8; google provider: 4.79.0
- Affected regions: us-west1 (primary), us-east1 (replica)

Steps to reproduce:
1. Checkout branch infra/cmek/onboard-analytics
2. Trigger CI workflow .github/workflows/provision-cmek.yaml or run `terraform apply` with service account impersonation from the runner.
3. Observe error during `google_kms_key_ring` creation step.

Observed logs / errors:
- iam.serviceAccounts.signBlob denied for impersonation attempts (logs attached in CI run).
- Error: "googleapi: Error 403: Permission 'resourcemanager.projects.create' denied by organization policy 'constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation'" (note: message varies depending on provider call).
- Audit log excerpt: orgpolicy.v1 DenyViolation on projects/redwood-prod-dedicated: constraint=constraints/gcp.restrictResourceCreation, denied_action=cloudkms.keyRings.create

Investigation notes:
- Org policy audit shows a recently-applied policy (GCP Org Policy: restrictResourceCreation -> permittedResources limited to team-managed projects) that blocks programmatic creation of KMS KeyRings unless the request originates from a whitelisted principal.
- CI runner uses impersonation of ci-service-sa to perform terraform; the org-policy expects either an approved infra-service account or a signed approval tag on the request.
- Cross-referenced with INT-572913 which flagged a similar deny pattern for a different project; that ticket included an org-policy change by Security on 2026-03-01.
- Hypothesis: Security applied a tightened policy during the compliance sweep; Dedicated onboarding path did not get the required exception.

Temporary mitigation / workaround:

title: Phased access ramp for contractor->employee conversion with device entitlement hold
preview:
Issue summary:
We have an external contractor converting to full-time employee (start date 2026-03-22). HR requests a phased access ramp: maintain contractor service accounts for a 2-week overlap but disable privileged groups until completion of background check and I-9. Device entitlement hold: current loaner MacBook must be retained by IT until device transfer checklist completed.

Impact:
Potential gap in access for cross-team handover; risk of orphaned service credentials if not rotated timely.

Context and constraints:
- Contractor uses several service accounts used in CI and internal datasets.
- Payroll/vendor sync must run prior to SSO provisioning for benefits.
- Need to ensure disk encryption keys and KMS access are transferred or rekeyed.

Requested changes:
1) Create bridge account (ephemeral) for two-week overlap with read-only access to production logs.
2) Schedule KMS rekey and rotate service-account keys during off-hours (window requested 2026-03-24 02:00-04:00 UTC).
3) Hold device entitlement: IT retains loaner laptop until hardware transfer checklist signed; create ticket for device reimage and company asset tag update.
4) Delay inclusion in 'privileged' LDAP groups until HR onboarding complete and manager confirms.
5) Produce short playbook for SRE and Security for this conversion flow.

Steps to reproduce/validate:
- HR confirms conversion in Greenhouse -> triggers SCIM event expected.
- Verify that bridge account is created with MFA enforced.
- Run KMS key rotation plan in staging, validate app connectivity.

Attachments:
- Offer letter redacted (link withheld)
- Current service account names: svc-ci-build, svc-data-sync (provided separately to SRE)

Notes:
This is time-sensitive to avoid payroll and benefits delays. Security requests minimal exposure during overlap.

title: After-hours staging room lockout during scheduled desk relocation - batch asset scan incomplete, temporary badge reissue and escorted retrieval required
preview: Issue summary: On 2026-03-10 during an after-hours desk relocation, the staging room (Floor 5, West wing) auto-locked while IT was mid-inventory. Impact: 28 devices (laptops, docking stations, and monitors) had incomplete barcode scans; several devices became inaccessible behind the locked door, preventing finish of asset transfer to storage. Location: Building 3 - Floor 5 West - Staging Room B. Steps to reproduce: 1) Schedule desk move outside of core hours. 2) Move assets to Staging Room B. 3) Attempt to finish batch scan when door controller triggers auto-lock due to scheduled lock at 19:00. Expected: Batch scan completes and assets are moved to storage with badges reactivated as needed. Actual: Door locked at 19:01 due to controller clock drift; scanner session terminated and 9 devices remained unscanned and behind locked door. Operational impact: Delayed onboarding for 3 hires, potential missing asset records until reconciliation.

title: Office AP power-save / DHCP rapid-renew causes WireGuard handshake to stall and internal service blackholes
preview: Multiple employees reported intermittent loss of internal services and failed WireGuard handshakes when switching between personal hotspot and corporate Wi‑Fi. Symptoms include long pauses during ssh/kshell connect, stalled LLM inference calls from internal tooling, and zero traffic observed through the WireGuard peer despite tunnel remaining logically up.
